description: This is a line plot illustrating the effect of Al addition (x-axis: Al content in wt.%, range 0 to 10) on Young's modulus (y-axis, unit GPa, range 140 to 220) of low-density ferrous alloys for automotive lightweighting applications. Two independent experimental datasets are presented: blue circular points with trendline from Rana et al. [6, 20], and red triangular points with trendline from Frommeyer et al. [18]. Both datasets exhibit a consistent monotonic decreasing trend of Young's modulus with increasing Al content: Rana et al.'s data shows Young's modulus reduces from ~205 GPa at 0 wt.% Al to ~153 GPa at 10 wt.% Al, while Frommeyer et al.'s data for 6 wt.% to ~8.5 wt.% Al shows modulus drops from ~190 GPa to ~167 GPa, matching the former trend. This figure verifies the negative side effect of Al alloying in low-density steels: while Al addition lowers steel density to enable vehicle weight reduction and improved fuel efficiency, it reduces Young's modulus which directly governs material stiffness, posing a challenge for application of these alloys in stiffness-critical automotive components.
